ficha.title: Technical details for selected indicators
ficha.click_to_view: Click to view in Technical Sheet

metodologia.title: Methodology
metodologia.subtitle: How AI analyses are generated
metodologia.intro: Each analysis is generated from real socioeconomic data, combined with an explicit editorial framing. The process is fully transparent: below we show how, from official data, and applying tailored instructions as a Lens, an LLM model (in 2026) produces tailored copy and editorial.
metodologia.instance_notice: This Prumo instance can run under any available lens. By default it uses a prompt generated by its authors. Try the available lenses to see the same data interpreted from different political perspectives.
metodologia.step1_sub: Over 350 indicators from 9 official sources, covering 54 countries (EU-27, PALOP and global economies). Series since 1960 updated automatically via public APIs.
metodologia.step2_sub: Injected at the start of each prompt. Defines the analysis viewpoint — selectable and transparent. Each lens is derived from the respective party's electoral programme.
metodologia.step3_sub: Data from step 1 and framing from step 2 are combined into a single structured prompt. The model can also search the web for recent news context.
metodologia.step4_sub: The model returns structured text with analysis, framing and context links. It is presented as an expandable card next to the series chart.
metodologia.custom_placeholder: Write your custom ideological framing here…
metodologia.custom_hint: Text sent as context to the AI model. Saved in browser.
metodologia.custom_placeholder_painel: Write your ideological framing here. E.g.: 'You are an analyst who prioritises environmental sustainability and the circular economy…'
metodologia.custom_hint_painel: This text is sent as context to the AI model. Saved locally in browser.

ficha.disclaimer: The data presented is compiled from official sources for informational and analytical purposes. It does not replace official publications from the source organisations. For primary data, consult INE, Eurostat, Bank of Portugal and World Bank.

seasonal.title: Seasonal Adjustment
seasonal.body: Many economic series have regularly repeating patterns (e.g., industrial production drops in August due to holidays). Seasonal adjustment removes these predictable patterns using the TRAMO-SEATS method, employed by INE and Eurostat. Series marked '(seas. adj.)' are treated this way, enabling month-to-month comparisons without seasonal distortion.

base_index.title: Temporal Base (Indices)
base_index.body: Index indicators (such as IPI or HICP) are expressed relative to a base year (e.g., 2021=100). For correct comparisons, it is essential to verify that indices use the same base. Prumo always normalises to the base published by the official source.

comparability.title: European Comparability
comparability.body: When comparing Portugal with the EU, we use Eurostat harmonised data whenever available. The HICP (Harmonised Index of Consumer Prices) is preferred over the national CPI because it is comparable across countries. The unemployment rate follows the ILO definition via the Labour Force Survey.

gdp_differential.title: GDP per Capita Differential Calculation
gdp_differential.body: The PT vs EU convergence differential is calculated on GDP per capita in PPP (Purchasing Power Parity), which adjusts for cost-of-living differences between countries. A rising differential indicates real convergence — Portugal is catching up with the European average.

eu27.title: EU27_2020 Aggregate
eu27.body: EU27_2020 is the Eurostat aggregate for the current 27 EU Member States, following the UK's departure (Brexit, February 2020). Eurostat retroactively recalculates historical series excluding the UK, ensuring temporal consistency even for periods before 2020.

update_freq.title: Update Frequency by Source
update_freq.ine_desc: CPI, IPI, Employment, GDP, Foreign trade
update_freq.bportugal_desc: Euribor, Sovereign spread, Balance of payments
update_freq.worldbank_desc: GDP, Life expectancy, Employment, Health, Education

disclaimer.title: Disclaimer
disclaimer.body: The data presented is compiled from official sources for informational and analytical purposes. Prumo PT is not an official publication and does not replace the original data. AI-generated analyses are for informational purposes only and reflect the selected editorial framing — they do not constitute economic or financial advice.

interpretation.title: Interpretation Notes
interpretation.ipi: The IPI (Industrial Production Index) is a leading indicator of economic activity. Consecutive drops may anticipate GDP deceleration.
interpretation.eu27_avg: The EU27 average can mask large disparities between countries. Always compare with relevant peers (Southern Europe, Visegrad Group, etc.).
interpretation.employment: The employment rate and the unemployment rate measure different things: an inactive person is not counted as unemployed.
interpretation.long_series: Long series (>20 years) may have breaks due to methodological changes. Eurostat flags these in the metadata.
interpretation.nominal: Nominal values (EUR) are not adjusted for inflation. For long-term comparisons, prefer real or PPP indicators.
interpretation.worldbank_lag: World Bank data has a 1–2 year lag. For more recent data, prefer high-frequency sources (INE, Eurostat, FRED).

about.title: About Prumo PT
about.embed_desc: Copy the snippet below to embed an interactive Prumo PT chart in your site or article.

what_is.title: What is Prumo PT
what_is.intro: Portuguese economy dashboard with data from 9 official sources and AI analysis.
what_is.indicators: {{count}}+ indicators — production, employment, prices, energy, foreign trade
what_is.sources: Sources: INE, Eurostat, Bank of Portugal, OECD, World Bank, REN, DGEG, ERSE, FRED
what_is.ai_analysis: Interpretive analysis generated by LLM, with selectable editorial lens
what_is.disclaimer: Not an official publication — it is a working tool

painel.title: Dashboard
painel.body: Portugal overview: 30+ indicators organised by 7 themes — Cost of Living, Industry, Employment, Economic Outlook, Energy, External and Competitiveness. Each card shows the latest value, trend and year-on-year change.
painel.tip_click: Click any card to open the indicator in the Analysis section.
painel.tip_ia: The ✦ AI button at the top generates interpretive analysis of all themes, with related reading per section.

comparativos.title: Comparisons
comparativos.body: Compare Portugal with all EU and world countries across dozens of indicators. Combines Eurostat data (EU27, high frequency) with the World Bank (global coverage, annual) in a single interface.
comparativos.tip_lines: Lines: temporal evolution of all selected countries. Hover over the legend to highlight a series.
comparativos.tip_snapshot: Snapshot: bar chart with the latest available value, sorted by country.
comparativos.tip_composite: Indicators with ★ are composite: Eurostat for EU27 + World Bank for the rest.
comparativos.tip_grey: Countries without data for the selected indicator appear greyed out and are not selectable.
comparativos.tip_lag: World Bank data has a 1–2 year lag; Eurostat, 1–3 months.

analise.title: Analysis
analise.body: Free time series explorer. Search any indicator from 9 available sources, adjust the time range and view the chart. AI interpretation adapts focus to the horizon.
analise.tip_multi: You can select and overlay multiple indicators simultaneously.
analise.tip_dual_axis: When two indicators have incompatible units, the chart automatically uses two vertical axes.
analise.tip_csv: CSV button exports raw data for external analysis.
analise.tip_share: Share button copies the URL with the current chart state.
analise.tip_deeplink: Deep-link from the Technical Sheet: click on any indicator code.

metodologia.title: Methodology and Technical Sheet
metodologia.body: The Methodology section explains how each AI analysis is generated — real data, transparent editorial framing and complete prompt. The Technical Sheet documents all sources and indicators.
metodologia.tip_click_code: Clicking on any indicator code takes you directly to the Analysis section.
metodologia.tip_multi_country: Indicators with multi-country coverage (e.g. Eurostat by nation) open in Comparisons.
metodologia.tip_methodology: The Methodology section explains seasonal adjustment, index bases and comparability.

realtime.a: No. Data is updated periodically from the official publications of each source (INE, Eurostat, Bank of Portugal, etc.). The lag varies by indicator: the IPI is released ~2 months after the reference month; quarterly GDP, ~3 months after the quarter; World Bank data arrives with a 1–2 year delay. The date of the last update is shown at the top of the Dashboard.

seasonal.a: Many economic series have patterns that repeat regularly — industrial production always drops in August due to holidays, for example. Seasonal adjustment removes these predictable patterns so that consecutive months can be compared without distortion. Series with '(seas. adj.)' are treated this way. The method used by INE and Eurostat is TRAMO-SEATS.

report.q: Can I use this data in a report?
report.a: The data comes from official public sources (INE, Eurostat, Bank of Portugal, etc.). You may use it by citing the corresponding primary source. Prumo PT is not an official publication and does not replace the original data. For primary data, consult each entity directly.

eu27.a: It is the Eurostat aggregate for the current 27 EU Member States after the UK's departure (Brexit, February 2020). Eurostat retroactively recalculates historical series excluding the UK — so the 'EU27_2020' series is consistent across all years, even for periods before 2020.

ai.q: How does the AI analysis work?
ai.a: The Analysis section generates an interpretive paragraph from the data visible in the chart, with web search to suggest related recent readings. The focus adapts to the time horizon: 1 year — short-term analysis; 5 years — business cycle; 10 years — structural trend. All AI-generated content is filtered through the selected ideological lens.

grey_countries.q: Why do some countries appear grey in Comparisons?
grey_countries.a: Grey countries have no data available in the database for the selected indicator. This happens because not all sources cover all countries: Eurostat mainly covers EU27; the World Bank has global coverage but with gaps in sector-specific series.

composite.a: For some indicators of high comparative interest (such as Unemployment and Employment Rate), we combine two sources: Eurostat for EU27 countries and the World Bank for the rest of the world. The result is a single, coherent series with worldwide coverage.

error.q: I found an error or have a suggestion. What do I do?
error.a: Send an email to the author with a description of the problem (which indicator, what value seems wrong, what you would expect to see). Data errors are a priority — thank you for helping improve Prumo PT.

author.title: Author
author.body: Created by Joao Peixoto, PMP — project manager and engineer. AI-assisted development (Claude, Anthropic); all editorial and architectural decisions are the author's. Open-source code (AGPL-3.0) — will be published on GitHub when stabilised.

noscript.title: Prumo PT — Economy by the Numbers
noscript.message: This dashboard requires JavaScript to load data and charts.

kpi_descriptions.inflation: Consumer price variation (HICP). Measures the erosion of purchasing power of wages and savings.
kpi_descriptions.diesel: Average retail price. Main fuel for freight transport and agriculture.
kpi_descriptions.gasoline_95: Gasoline 95 at the pump — paired with diesel for a complete reading of mobility costs.
kpi_descriptions.electricity_btn: Simple tariff in Low Voltage — benchmark for households and small businesses.
kpi_descriptions.electricity_mt: Medium Voltage network access tariff (peak hour) — benchmark for industrial SMEs.
kpi_descriptions.electricity_at: High Voltage network access tariff (peak hour) — benchmark for large industries.
kpi_descriptions.euribor_3m: Euribor 3 months — key driver of variable-rate mortgage repayments with quarterly review.
kpi_descriptions.euribor_6m: Euribor 6 months — benchmark for mortgages with semi-annual review.
kpi_descriptions.euribor_12m: Euribor 12 months — rate used in loan contracts with annual review.
kpi_descriptions.ipi_total: General barometer of national industrial production. Base 2021=100. Seasonally adjusted series by INE.
kpi_descriptions.ipi_cae_10: Food Industry (CAE 10) — Portugal's largest industrial sector; includes dairy, canned goods, industrial pastry.
kpi_descriptions.ipi_cae_20: Chemicals (CAE 20) — fertilisers, plastics, paints, basic pharmaceuticals. Industrial inputs indicator.
kpi_descriptions.ipi_cae_24: Basic Metals (CAE 24) — steel, aluminium, copper and alloys. Base for construction and manufacturing.
kpi_descriptions.ipi_cae_25: Metal Products (CAE 25) — structures, packaging, tools and fabricated metal components.
kpi_descriptions.ipi_cae_28: Machinery & Equipment (CAE 28) — capital goods for industry; indicator of productive investment capacity.
kpi_descriptions.ipi_cae_29: Motor Vehicles (CAE 29) — key export sector for Portugal (Autoeuropa, Caetano Bus).
kpi_descriptions.copper: Copper — global economy barometer; essential for construction, electronics and electrification.
kpi_descriptions.aluminum: Aluminium — cross-cutting structural metal: automotive, packaging, construction. Industrial demand indicator.
kpi_descriptions.unemployment: Harmonised unemployment rate (OECD). Percentage of labour force without a job and seeking work.
kpi_descriptions.industrial_employment: Employment volume index in manufacturing (CAE C). Base 2021=100, seasonally adjusted.
kpi_descriptions.wages_industry: Nominal wages index in industry. Not inflation-adjusted — for real wages, compare with HICP.
kpi_descriptions.cli: OECD composite leading indicator. Anticipates business cycle turning points ~6 months ahead.
kpi_descriptions.confidence: Balance of positive vs negative responses from manufacturers (INE). Below zero indicates prevailing pessimism.
kpi_descriptions.order_books: Order books balance from industrial firms (BTS/OECD). Indicates future production demand.
kpi_descriptions.energy_cost: MIBEL wholesale price in the Iberian market. Benchmark for industrial tariffs and long-term contracts.
kpi_descriptions.renewable_share: Renewable share of electricity production (DGEG). Portugal has one of Europe's best profiles.
kpi_descriptions.energy_dependence: Percentage of total energy consumption that is imported. High dependence means vulnerability to external prices.
kpi_descriptions.natural_gas: Natural Gas — critical for energy-intensive industries (glass, ceramics, metallurgy) and electricity production.
kpi_descriptions.solar: Photovoltaic production injected into the grid (REN). Indicator of energy transition and installed solar capacity.
kpi_descriptions.wind: Wind production injected into the grid (REN). Portugal ranks among European leaders in wind capacity per capita.
kpi_descriptions.eur_usd: Euro/dollar exchange rate. Influences export competitiveness and USD-denominated import prices.
kpi_descriptions.spread_pt_de: 10-year PT-DE yield spread. Measures sovereign risk perceived by markets relative to Germany.
kpi_descriptions.brent: European benchmark crude. Directly influences diesel, gasoline, petrochemicals and transport.
kpi_descriptions.gdp_per_capita: Average wealth per inhabitant. PT: ~23,500 EUR vs EU27: ~34,000 EUR — structural convergence gap.
kpi_descriptions.rnd_pct_gdp: R&D intensity (% GDP). PT: 1.7% vs EU27: 2.3% (Horizon Europe target: 3%). Long-term disadvantage.
kpi_descriptions.employment_rate: Employment rate (% pop. 20-64 with a job). Broad measure of the labour market.

kpi_explains.inflation: When inflation rises, the same salary buys less. At 2.3%, a 500 EUR basket in January costs 511.50 EUR by December.
kpi_explains.diesel: Diesel prices affect everything — from bread to transport. When they rise, prices of almost everything else rise too.
kpi_explains.gasoline_95: The gasoline you pump at the station. If you spend 50 EUR per tank, a 10% rise means 5 EUR more per week.
kpi_explains.electricity_btn: Your home electricity bill tariff. If it rises, you pay more at the end of the month even using the same amount.
kpi_explains.electricity_mt: Tariff for medium-sized factories and shops. When it rises, production costs increase and consumer prices may follow.
kpi_explains.electricity_at: Tariff for the country's largest industries. Affects the cost of producing steel, glass, ceramics and other heavy sectors.
kpi_explains.euribor_3m: The 3-month Euribor is the rate banks use to calculate your mortgage payment. If you have a variable-rate loan, when this rises, your monthly payment rises.
kpi_explains.euribor_6m: Similar to 3-month Euribor, but for loans with semi-annual review. Changes every 6 months — if you have this type of loan, your payment adjusts twice a year.
kpi_explains.euribor_12m: For loans with annual review. Your payment only changes once a year, but the variation can be larger each time.
kpi_explains.ipi_total: Measures whether Portuguese factories are producing more or less. When it rises, there is more activity, more employment and more exports.
kpi_explains.ipi_cae_10: The food industry is Portugal's largest industrial sector. If it falls, it may mean less supply and higher prices at the supermarket.
kpi_explains.ipi_cae_20: The chemical industry makes paints, plastics and fertilisers. It is a barometer of the health of other industries that depend on these materials.
kpi_explains.ipi_cae_24: Basic metals — the steel and aluminium used in construction and industry. If it falls, it may signal fewer construction projects and less investment.
kpi_explains.ipi_cae_25: Metal structures, tools and components. This is what transforms raw metal into useful parts for construction and industry.
kpi_explains.ipi_cae_28: Machinery and equipment for factories. If companies buy more machines, they are investing in the future — a good sign for the economy.
kpi_explains.ipi_cae_29: Car production (Autoeuropa, Caetano Bus) is one of Portugal's largest exports. When it rises, more money comes into the country.
kpi_explains.copper: Copper is used in everything from electrical cables to smartphones. Its price is considered a barometer of the global economy.
kpi_explains.aluminum: Aluminium is in cans, windows, cars and planes. When the price rises, products that use it become more expensive.
kpi_explains.unemployment: The percentage of people who want to work but cannot find a job. The lower, the better for families.
kpi_explains.industrial_employment: How many people work in Portuguese factories. If it rises, industry is hiring; if it falls, it is cutting jobs.
kpi_explains.wages_industry: How much industrial workers earn on average. Note that this value does not discount inflation — if prices rise more than wages, purchasing power is lost.
kpi_explains.cli: An indicator that tries to predict whether the economy will improve or worsen over the next 6 months. Above 100 suggests growth ahead.
kpi_explains.confidence: Factory owners are asked if they are optimistic or pessimistic. Below zero, most are pessimistic — a bad sign for investment and employment.
kpi_explains.order_books: The orders that factories have on their books. More orders today mean more production and more employment in the coming months.
kpi_explains.energy_cost: The wholesale electricity price in the Iberian market. When it rises, industrial tariffs and eventually household tariffs tend to follow.
kpi_explains.renewable_share: What share of electricity comes from sun, wind and water. The higher, the less we depend on imported fuels and the less CO2 we emit.
kpi_explains.energy_dependence: What percentage of the energy we consume comes from abroad. The higher, the more vulnerable we are to international price crises.
kpi_explains.natural_gas: Natural gas heats homes, powers glass and ceramics factories, and produces electricity. When the price rises, the entire cost chain rises.
kpi_explains.solar: How much solar electricity entered the grid. More solar means less gas and oil imports, and potentially lower electricity bills.
kpi_explains.wind: How much electricity wind farms injected into the grid. Portugal is one of the European countries with the most usable wind.
kpi_explains.eur_usd: When the euro rises against the dollar, US imports become cheaper, but our exports become more expensive for Americans.
kpi_explains.spread_pt_de: Measures how much more Portugal pays in interest than Germany. The higher, the more markets consider Portugal a risk — and the more expensive public debt becomes.
kpi_explains.brent: The price of a barrel of oil. Directly affects what you pay for diesel, gasoline and the transport of everything you buy.
kpi_explains.gdp_per_capita: The average wealth produced per inhabitant. Portugal is still below the European average — closing this gap is the great long-term challenge.
kpi_explains.rnd_pct_gdp: How much the country invests in research and innovation. More investment today means better jobs and more competitive products tomorrow.
kpi_explains.employment_rate: The percentage of adults (20-64) who have a job. The higher, the more people contribute to the economy and to Social Security.

inflation.deflation: Deflation — prices are falling (rare and not always good).
inflation.high: High inflation — significant pressure on households.

diesel.expensive: Expensive diesel — weighing on transport and prices.
diesel.very_expensive: Very expensive diesel — strong impact on costs.

gasoline_95.expensive: Expensive gasoline — each tank costs more.
gasoline_95.very_expensive: Very expensive gasoline — strong impact on mobility.

euribor_3m.negative: Negative rate — rare scenario, good for borrowers.
euribor_3m.low: Low rate — mortgage payments at their minimum.
euribor_3m.moderate: Moderate rate — payments rising for many families.
euribor_3m.high: High rate — mortgage payments weighing on budgets.
euribor_3m.very_high: Very high rate — strong pressure on borrowers.

unemployment.full_employment: Full employment — very tight labour market.
unemployment.moderate: Moderate unemployment — still room for improvement.

employment_rate.high: High rate — above the EU average.
employment_rate.reasonable: Reasonable rate — close to the European average.
employment_rate.below_avg: Below EU average — room for improvement.
employment_rate.low: Low rate — many adults out of the labour market.

cli.strong_expansion: Above 101 — strong signal of economic expansion ahead.
cli.contraction: Below 99 — signal of economic contraction ahead.

confidence.deep_pessimism: Deep pessimism — companies holding back investment.

order_books.above_normal: Orders above normal — future production secured.
order_books.weak: Weak order book — production may slow down.
order_books.very_weak: Very weak orders — signal of industrial recession.

renewable_share.excellent: Excellent — over 70% renewable, among Europe's best.
renewable_share.good: Good renewable share — above half of production.
renewable_share.moderate: Moderate share — still heavily dependent on fossils.
renewable_share.low: Low renewable share — strong dependence on imports.

energy_dependence.moderate: Moderate dependence — typical level for Portugal.
energy_dependence.high: High dependence — vulnerable to price shocks.

natural_gas.cheap: Cheap gas — good for energy-intensive industries.
natural_gas.expensive: Expensive gas — pressure on industrial costs.
natural_gas.very_expensive: Very expensive gas — severe impact on glass, ceramics and metallurgy factories.

spread_pt_de.very_low: Very low spread — markets trust Portugal.
spread_pt_de.moderate: Moderate spread — some caution from markets.
spread_pt_de.high: High spread — state borrowing costs rising.

brent.cheap: Cheap oil — good for importers like Portugal.
brent.expensive: Expensive oil — pressure on fuels and transport.
brent.very_expensive: Very expensive oil — widespread impact on prices.

eur_usd.strong_euro: Strong euro — cheaper imports, less competitive exports.
eur_usd.weak_euro: Weak euro — more competitive exports, more expensive imports.
eur_usd.very_weak_euro: Very weak euro — energy and raw materials (priced in USD) become expensive.

ipi_total.strong_contraction: Production well below base — strong industrial contraction.

gdp_per_capita.converging: Above 30,000 EUR — approaching the EU average.
gdp_per_capita.below_avg: Between 23-30k EUR — below the EU average, but converging.
gdp_per_capita.significant_gap: Below 23,000 EUR — significant gap versus the European average.

rnd_pct_gdp.strong: Strong R&D investment — above the EU average.
rnd_pct_gdp.moderate: Moderate R&D investment — below the European target of 3%.
rnd_pct_gdp.low: Low R&D investment — risk of losing competitiveness over time.
